title: End of module: Thermal Physics and Ideal Gases

questions.text: Convert a temperature of $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ to kelvin.
The internal energy of a system is best described as…
choices: the total kinetic energy of its molecules only
the sum of the randomly distributed kinetic and potential energies of its molecules
the total heat that has been supplied to it
the average temperature of its molecules
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the sum of the random distribution of kinetic energy (from particle motion) and potential energy (from particle separations/bonds) of all the particles in a system.

questions.text: Calculate the energy required to raise the temperature of $0.40\,\text{kg}$ of water from $15\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $55\,^\circ\text{C}$. Take the specific heat capacity of water as $4200\,\text{J}\,\text{kg}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$.

The specific latent heat of vaporisation of water is $2.26\times10^6\,\text{J}\,\text{kg}^{-1}$. Calculate the energy needed to boil away $0.150\,\text{kg}$ of water that is already at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$.
While a pure substance is changing state (e.g. melting), and energy continues to be transferred to it, its temperature…
questions.explanation: During a change of state, all of the energy supplied goes into changing the potential energy of the particles (breaking/forming bonds), not their kinetic energy, so the temperature stays constant until the change of state is complete.

questions.text: A cylinder contains $0.0800\,\text{mol}$ of an ideal gas at a temperature of $310\,\text{K}$ and a pressure of $2.00\times10^5\,\text{Pa}$. Calculate the volume of the gas. Take $R = 8.31\,\text{J}\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$.

A container of volume $2.50\times10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$ holds gas at a pressure of $1.01\times10^5\,\text{Pa}$ and a temperature of $300\,\text{K}$. Using $pV = NkT$ with $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}\,\text{J}\,\text{K}^{-1}$, calculate the number of molecules $N$ present.
Which of the following are correct assumptions of the kinetic theory model of an ideal gas?
choices: The volume of the molecules themselves is negligible compared with the volume of the container
Intermolecular forces are negligible except during collisions
Collisions between molecules are perfectly inelastic
The duration of a collision is negligible compared with the time between collisions
All molecules move at exactly the same speed
questions.explanation: Kinetic theory assumes molecules occupy negligible volume, exert negligible forces on each other except while colliding, and spend negligible time in each collision compared with the time between collisions. Collisions are assumed **elastic** (not inelastic), and molecular speeds are randomly distributed, not identical.

questions.text: Calculate the mean translational kinetic energy of a gas molecule at a temperature of $350\,\text{K}$. Take $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}\,\text{J}\,\text{K}^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: From $\tfrac12 m\langle c^2\rangle = \tfrac32 kT$, the mean kinetic energy is $\tfrac32 kT = 1.5 \times 1.38\times10^{-23} \times 350 = 7.245\times10^{-21}\,\text{J}$.

questions.text: If the absolute temperature of a fixed mass of gas is doubled, what happens to the mean kinetic energy of its molecules? (Answer in one or two words, e.g. "it doubles".)
questions.explanation: Mean kinetic energy $\tfrac32 kT$ is directly proportional to absolute temperature $T$, so doubling $T$ doubles the mean kinetic energy of the molecules.
